src:source.src:title: DocBook: The Definitive Guide, Version 5.2
src:source.src:overview: Reference guide for DocBook 5.2 document structures. Its lists of informal elements, list elements, publishing elements, synopsis elements, technical elements, and verbatim elements inform the Clayers prose vocabulary expansion.
src:source.src:author: Norman Walsh

pr:section.pr:title: Technical Documentation Prose
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Richer DITA/DocBook-inspired structures for writing task-oriented, reference-heavy, and diagnostics-aware technical documentation in the prose layer.
pr:section.pr:p: The original prose layer intentionally started small: sections, paragraphs, lists, steps, notes, code blocks, tables, and cross-references. That vocabulary is enough for architectural specification, but it is too thin for complete technical documentation. Real documentation needs precise task steps, examples, figures, content-backed media, definition lists, property tables, and synopses.

DITA's technical-content vocabulary provides the strongest model for task authoring: prerequisites, context, command text, step information, substeps, examples, step results, task results, and post-requisites. Meaningful choices belong in the deliberation layer, and troubleshooting belongs in the diagnostic layer. DocBook complements the prose model with long-form publishing structures such as formal examples, figures, variable lists, block quotes, sidebars, and command/API synopses. Clayers should adopt the useful authoring shapes without copying either standard's full specialization system.

The prose layer remains the right home for these constructs. They are writing structures, not separate semantic dimensions. Organization still classifies nodes as concept, task, or reference; relation still captures semantic edges; source still records provenance. The prose layer supplies the document grammar authors use inside those nodes.
General prose documentation therefore includes these authoring capabilities:

pr:ul.pr:li: Richer inline phrases for user-interface labels, keyboard input, file paths, command names, parameter names, API names, variables, message text, quotations, superscript, and subscript.
Definition lists for term/description pairs, matching DocBook's variable-list use case and common API/configuration documentation.

Examples, figures, and content-backed media references so documentation can show concrete outputs, screenshots, diagrams, and other supporting assets while reusing cnt:content identity, media type, URL, and hash metadata.
Property tables for reference topics, with type, value, and description columns inspired by DITA reference properties.
Structured task elements for prerequisite, context, command, supplemental information, substeps, step examples, step results, task result, and post-requisite content.
Synopses, block quotes, and sidebars for command/API summaries, quoted material, and secondary explanatory material.
Related-links blocks that provide explicit link lists in addition to inline cross-references.

pr:section.pr:p: These constructs are intentionally authoring-level elements. They do not replace terminology, source citations, content references, diagnostic issues, deliberation choice sets, artifact mappings, or relations. Inline term references and citations continue to work inside prose content, and the postprocessor should synthesize relation edges from prose cross-references, related links, and content-backed media.

pr:section.pr:title: Technical Documentation XSLT Processing
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Rendering and semantic postprocessing required for the expanded prose vocabulary.
pr:section.pr:p: The documentation renderer should translate new prose structures into stable HTML: examples and figures become titled blocks, media resolves through content nodes when possible, definition lists become HTML definition lists, properties become tables, task details become labeled sections, diagnostic issues become labeled remediation cards, deliberation choice sets become option cards, and related links become navigable link lists.

Each rendered section should also expose its underlying XML through a compact source button. The renderer stores serialized section XML in an inert HTML template and reads the template content when the popup opens. Activating the button opens a scrollable XML browser with collapsible element structure, highlighted names, attributes, and text, and a copy action for the original source. The browser view normalizes whitespace in text nodes for readability, wraps long text values within a readable measure, and wraps long attribute lists across multiple rows. This keeps prose-heavy nodes and namespace-heavy root elements readable without forcing excessively wide modal content. It preserves the exact serialized XML for copying. This makes the generated HTML useful as both documentation and an inspection surface for the source specification.

The generated HTML should prefer guided entry points when no fragment identifier is present in the URL. On initial load without a hash, the first reading map should be selected and the sidebar should keep the Reading Maps group open while collapsing all groups below it. Those lower sidebar groups should be emitted with their collapsed state already present in the initial HTML so page load does not briefly flash the expanded navigation. On initial load with a hash, the renderer should activate the page or in-page node named by the hash, open only the sidebar group that contains that target, and preserve the hash target for history and highlighting.
The interactive graph should zoom around the mouse cursor position so wheel gestures keep the point under the cursor stable instead of zooming toward the graph origin.

The combined-document postprocessor should synthesize rel:relation type="references" edges from pr:xref and related-link entries. This makes prose cross-references visible to connectivity, query, and graph tooling instead of leaving them as presentation-only links.

pr:section.pr:title: Technical Documentation Example
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: A compact example that exercises the expanded prose vocabulary in the self-spec.
pr:section.pr:p: This example demonstrates how a topic can combine reference data, task instructions, examples, media, diagnostics, and deliberation while still linking to the design in .

pr:dlentry.pr:dd: DITA/DocBook-inspired XML elements for technical writing.
XSLT templates that turn prose elements into stable HTML.

pr:property.pr:propdesc: Marks file-system paths without relying on generic code spans.
Marks commands and subcommands in procedural documentation.

pr:prereq.pr:p: The prose schema must include the expanded task, reference, and publishing constructs before this example can validate.

pr:step.pr:cmd: Run cargo run -p clayers -- validate clayers/clayers/.

pr:info.pr:p: The command validates both legacy prose and the new rich documentation structures.

pr:example.pr:title: Related link synthesis
pr:example.pr:p: A related link below should become a synthesized references relation in the combined document.

pr:figure.pr:title: Documentation screenshot asset
pr:figure.pr:caption: Media references can point at repository assets and carry required alternative text.

diag:issue.diag:title: Validation fails after adding a prose element
diag:issue.diag:condition: The validator reports that a prose child is not accepted by the model group.
diag:issue.diag:cause: The element is either outside its allowed context or missing from schemas/prose.xsd.
diag:issue.diag:remedy: Move the element to an allowed context, or move domain data to the appropriate layer before using it in spec content.

delib:option.delib:title: Keep authoring structures in prose
delib:option.delib:rationale: Sections, steps, examples, figures, sidebars, and synopses are documentation grammar.

delib:option.delib:title: Move reusable media, troubleshooting, and meaningful choices to domain layers
delib:option.delib:rationale: Content identity, failure modes, and tradeoffs need queryable semantics beyond presentation.

pln:plan.pln:title: Technical documentation prose expansion
pln:plan.pln:overview: Add DITA/DocBook-inspired authoring structures to the prose schema, render them in HTML documentation, and synthesize reference relations from prose links.

pln:item.pln:title: Extend prose schema
pln:item.pln:description: Add rich inline phrases, examples, figures, media, definition lists, properties, structured task details, synopses, block quotes, sidebars, and related links; move reusable media, troubleshooting, and meaningful choices to their semantic layers.

pln:acceptance.pln:criterion: The shipped prose schema validates the new authoring structures; witness command: cargo run -p clayers -- validate clayers/clayers/.

pln:item.pln:title: Add XSLT support
pln:item.pln:description: Render the new prose elements in documentation HTML and synthesize reference relations from prose cross-references. Include an XML source browser for rendered sections.

pln:acceptance.pln:criterion: Documentation generation succeeds with the expanded prose vocabulary; witness command: cargo run -p clayers -- doc clayers/clayers/ --output /tmp/clayers-doc.html.

pln:item.pln:title: Improve generated HTML navigation ergonomics
pln:item.pln:description: Default no-hash loads to the first reading map with lower sidebar groups emitted as collapsed, keep hash-target navigation focused on the containing sidebar group, and preserve hash targets in history and highlighting.

pln:acceptance.pln:criterion: Documentation generation succeeds and the generated markup and JavaScript contain hash-aware sidebar initialization, initially collapsed lower sidebar groups, and target-preserving history; witness command: cargo run -p clayers -- doc clayers/clayers/ --output /tmp/clayers-doc.html.

pln:item.pln:title: Improve generated HTML graph zoom ergonomics
pln:item.pln:description: Make graph wheel zoom preserve the graph point under the mouse cursor.

pln:acceptance.pln:criterion: Documentation generation succeeds and the generated JavaScript contains cursor-centered graph wheel zoom; witness command: cargo run -p clayers -- doc clayers/clayers/ --output /tmp/clayers-doc.html.

org:concept.org:purpose: Explains why the prose layer needs richer DITA/DocBook-inspired authoring structures for full technical documentation.
Defines rendering and postprocessing behavior for the expanded prose vocabulary.
Provides a self-spec example that validates and renders the expanded prose authoring vocabulary.

org:task.org:purpose: Tracks implementation work for the prose schema and XSLT expansion.
Tracks generated HTML graph zoom improvements.

rel:relation.rel:note: Richer prose remains part of the layered architecture as an authoring grammar rather than a separate semantic dimension.
XSLT rendering and reference synthesis complete the authoring vocabulary by making it visible in docs and graph tooling.
The example exercises the prose constructs specified by the general technical documentation prose node.
DITA technical content informs task, reference, and diagnostic and deliberation structures.
DocBook informs examples, figures, definition lists, publishing blocks, synopses, and verbatim structures.
The XSLT support depends on the prose schema admitting the new elements.
The plan operationalizes the technical documentation prose expansion.
The navigation quality-of-life item operationalizes the generated HTML behavior specified for reading-map landing pages, hash-focused sidebar state, and target-preserving history.
The graph zoom quality-of-life item operationalizes the generated HTML behavior specified for cursor-centered graph wheel gestures.
The validation step is part of the technical documentation example.
The example figure uses a content-backed screenshot asset.
The diagnostic issue describes a prose validation failure mode.
The embedded issue demonstrates diagnostic-layer use inside technical documentation.
The choice set records where DITA-inspired structures belong.
The embedded choice set demonstrates deliberation-layer use inside technical documentation.
The selected option belongs to the prose/domain choice set.
The selected option belongs to the prose/domain choice set.

spec:clayers.llm:node: Technical documentation prose expansion: explains why the initial DITA-style prose layer needs richer structures for complete technical documentation. It cites DITA for task/reference authoring and DocBook for examples, figures, variable lists, publishing blocks, synopses, and verbatim structures. It also enumerates the prose vocabulary additions: richer inline phrases, definition lists, examples, figures, content-backed media, property tables, structured task details, synopses, block quotes, sidebars, and related links. Troubleshooting belongs to the diagnostic layer, and meaningful choices belong to the deliberation layer.

Specifies XSLT processing for expanded prose: render the new authoring structures into HTML and synthesize rel:relation type="references" from prose xrefs, related links, and content-backed media. Rendered sections expose a source button that opens a scrollable XML browser for inspecting the underlying spec node as a collapsible tree with highlighted element names, attributes, and text plus raw-source copy support. The generated HTML should load the first reading map when there is no URL hash, collapse lower sidebar groups in the initial markup, open only the containing sidebar group for hash-targeted loads, preserve hash targets in history, and zoom the graph around the mouse cursor.

Demonstrates the expanded prose vocabulary in the self-spec using definition lists, properties, task details, structured steps, examples, figures/media, diagnostic issues, deliberation choice sets, and related links.
Implementation plan for extending prose schema capabilities and adding XSLT rendering/postprocessing support for technical documentation.
Implementation plan item for generated HTML navigation ergonomics: reading-map default landing, hash-focused sidebar expansion, and target-preserving history.
Implementation plan item for generated HTML graph ergonomics: cursor-centered graph wheel zoom.
External source: DocBook: The Definitive Guide 5.2. Used as a reference for examples, figures, variable lists, publishing elements, synopsis elements, technical elements, and verbatim elements.

art:mapping.art:note: The prose schema implements the expanded technical-documentation vocabulary, including inline phrases, task structures, reference tables, examples, figures, content-backed media, sidebars, and related links.
Postprocessing support synthesizes references relations from prose xrefs, related links, and content-backed media references.
Documentation rendering templates for definition lists, task details, examples, figures, media, properties, publishing blocks, related links, and technical inline phrases.
Documentation CSS for the rendered technical-documentation blocks and inline keyboard styling.
HTML documentation source-browser support in the page shell: modal dialog behavior, parsed XML tree rendering, collapsible nodes, scrollable inspection, and raw-source copy action.

Generated HTML navigation quality-of-life support: reading-map default landing, hash-targeted sidebar focus, target-preserving history, and rehighlight and graph scope wrappers that forward target IDs.
Generated HTML graph wheel zoom support that keeps the graph point under the mouse cursor stable while scaling.
Section rendering support for XML source buttons and serialized source templates.

spec:clayers.art:exempt: External source declaration, not an implementation artifact.
Self-spec usage example for prose authoring structures; validation and rendering are mapped through the schema and XSLT nodes.
Example procedure step within documentation; not an implementation artifact.
Completed plan record; implementation mappings live on the schema and XSLT spec nodes.
Completed plan item represented by the prose schema mapping.
Completed plan item represented by the XSLT mappings.
Completed plan item represented by the generated-document XSLT mappings.
Completed plan item represented by the generated-document XSLT mappings.
